1: The theme of the "calling" in Christ as the new-covenant summons: Paul urges a walk "worthy of the calling you have received." The klēsis (calling) in Ephesians is always God's call in Christ (Ephesians 1:18; Ephesians 4:4); it is not a general religious summons but the specific event by which the Spirit effectually draws a person into union with Christ. Walking worthily is not earning the calling but living in correspondence with its nature — the nature of the one who called.
2: The theme of Christ's own character as the standard for community ethics: humility, gentleness, patience, and forbearing love describe the Christ who said "I am meek and lowly in heart" (Matthew 11:29) and who in Philippians 2:3-8 is the supreme example of humility. Paul does not appeal to an abstract virtue list; he is describing the character of the one in whom the Ephesians have been made alive, which the Spirit reproduces in those who belong to Christ.
3: The theme of Spirit-given unity as the fruit of Christ's peace-making work: the "bond of peace" that holds the unity of the Spirit is Christ's own peace (Ephesians 2:14-15) distributed through the Spirit. The peace that abolished the hostility between Jew and Gentile at the cross (Ephesians 2:16) is maintained in the community through the Spirit. The Ephesians are not creating unity; they are preserving what Christ has already established and the Spirit already sustains.
4: The theme of Trinitarian unity structured on the Shema: the seven-fold unity formula — one body, one Spirit, one hope, one Lord, one faith, one baptism, one God and Father — applies the Shema's monotheism ("The LORD our God, the LORD is one," Deuteronomy 6:4) to the new-covenant community. The "one hope" is eschatological — the return of Christ and the resurrection of the body — so the formula begins in the present (body, Spirit) and reaches toward the future Christ will bring.
5: A direct Christological claim: "one Lord" identifies Jesus as kyrios — the LXX title for YHWH applied to Christ throughout the NT. To confess one Lord in the context of the Shema's "one God" is to place Jesus within the divine identity. "One faith" is not subjective trust as a feeling but the gospel content that calls forth trust; "one baptism" is baptism into Christ's death and resurrection (Romans 6:3-4), the rite that enacts union with him.
6: A revelation of God: Christ has disclosed God as "Father of all" in a sense that exceeds the OT's national framework. The Father is "over all and through all and in all" — a pattern of divine omnipresence now specified as paternal. This universal fatherhood belongs to all who are "in Christ"; Paul has just been describing the community gathered in the one Lord. The God who is Father of all is known as such through the Son who reveals him (John 14:9).
7: A revelation of God: "grace has been given as Christ apportioned it" — the distribution of gifts is an act of the ascended Christ exercising lordship. The variety of gifts within the one body does not undermine the unity just described; it is Christ's deliberate distribution, matched to each person. The diversity of the body is not a problem to overcome but the design of the head — who exercises his lordship through differentiated gifting rather than uniform distribution.
8: A direct fulfillment: Psalms 68:18 is quoted — "When he ascended on high, he led captives in his train and gave gifts to his people." The psalm celebrates the divine warrior ascending Zion after victory, distributing the spoils of conquest. Paul applies the ascent to Christ's resurrection and ascension: the captives led in triumph are the powers defeated at the cross (Colossians 2:15), and the gifts distributed are the ministry gifts of verses 11-12. The triumphant king gives what he has won to those he has rescued.
9: The theme of Christ's complete downward and upward journey: "he also descended to the lower, earthly regions" — the incarnation and death of Christ constitute the descent that precedes the ascent. Christ's humiliation (Philippians 2:6-8) is the prerequisite for his exaltation (Philippians 2:9-11). The cosmos is filled with his presence only because he first emptied himself of heavenly glory to enter the lowest reaches of human experience — including death itself.
10: A revelation of God: "he who descended is the very one who ascended higher than all the heavens, in order to fill the whole universe." The ascended Christ fills (plērōsē) all things — the same verb as the "fullness" of 1:23 and Ephesians 3:19. The cosmic Christ whose presence pervades creation is not a philosophical abstraction but the same Jesus who descended into human flesh and death. The filling of all things is an extension of the incarnational pattern, not its abolition.
11: A direct Christological claim: "Christ himself gave the apostles, the prophets, the evangelists, the pastors and teachers." The ministry structure of the church is not a human institution; it is the ascended Christ's personal gift to his body. The gifts of Ephesians 4:7-8 take concrete form here as persons — Christ does not distribute abstract abilities but gives gifted people to his community. The church's leadership structure is Christologically structured from above, not administratively organized from below.
12: The theme of the body growing toward completion in Christ: "to equip his people for works of service, so that the body of Christ may be built up." The building/temple metaphor of Ephesians 2:21-22 continues: the church is still under construction, and the ministry gifts are the means of construction. The goal is not institutional growth but the building up of a body — a living organism whose head is Christ. Ministry equips people not for institutional roles but for participation in Christ's ongoing work.
13: A direct Christological claim: the goal of all ministry is "unity in the faith and in the knowledge of the Son of God" and "the whole measure of the fullness of Christ." Christ is both the destination and the measure. The helikia (stature, full measure) of Christ is the standard against which maturity is assessed; the church grows not toward abstract perfection but toward the full expression of what Christ is. He is the goal toward which the entire body moves.
14: The theme of doctrinal stability grounded in Christ: infants "tossed back and forth" by every wind of teaching are contrasted with those who know the Son of God (v13). False teaching exploits those who do not know Christ deeply; knowing Christ is the protection against the "cunning and craftiness" of deceivers. The instability Paul warns against is always a Christological instability — it begins when the center shifts away from Christ.
15: A direct Christological claim: "we will grow to become in every respect the mature body of him who is the head, that is, Christ." The head determines the direction and character of the body's growth. Growth in the body of Christ is not generic improvement but specific conformity to the head — the character, wisdom, and love of Christ permeating the body through truthful, loving speech. "Speaking the truth in love" is how Christ-shaped growth happens in community.
16: A direct Christological claim: "from him the whole body, joined and held together by every supporting ligament, grows and builds itself up in love, as each part does its work." Christ is the structural source — the preposition ex (from him) places him as the origin of all the body's growth. The love that drives the building up (v16) is Christ's own love (Ephesians 3:18-19; Ephesians 5:2), reproduced in the body through the Spirit. The body does not grow by its own energy but from its union with the head.
17: The theme of Christ as the boundary between old and new life: Gentile existence is characterized by "the futility of their thinking" (mataiótēs — the same word as Ecclesiastes' "vanity"). To live without Christ is to live in the same futility that the Preacher observed everywhere — life that circles endlessly without arriving at meaning. The Ephesians have been transferred out of that futility into a life ordered by the truth as it is in Jesus (v21).
18: A revelation of God: "darkened in their understanding and separated from the life of God because of the ignorance that is in them." The condition of those without Christ is not merely moral failure but ontological separation from the source of life. Christ, who said "I am the life" (John 14:6) and "the light of the world" (John 8:12), is the one whose presence reverses the darkness and separation. The verse defines the darkness that Christ's coming addressed.
19: The theme of the end-state of life without Christ: "having lost all sensitivity, they have given themselves over to sensuality so as to indulge in every kind of impurity." The deadening of conscience and the escalation of appetite describe what happens when human beings are not anchored in the living God. Paul is not moralizing; he is diagnosing. The trajectory ends in total loss of self-governance — the opposite of the freedom Christ brings (John 8:36; 2 Corinthians 3:17).
20: A direct Christological claim: "you, however, did not come to know Christ that way." The phrase emathete ton Christon — "you learned Christ" — is unique in Paul: Christ himself is the content of Christian formation, not merely its teacher or exemplar. To be discipled is not to learn propositions about Christ but to learn Christ — to have him as the shaping reality of one's mind and life. This is the most direct expression in Ephesians of Christ as the new-humanity curriculum.
21: A direct Christological claim: "if indeed you heard about him and were taught in him in accordance with the truth that is in Jesus." The rare use of "Jesus" without a title grounds the ethical exhortation in the historical person — his teaching, his life, the pattern he embodied in Galilee and Judea. The "truth as it is in Jesus" is not an abstraction but a life, a set of practices, a character that can be heard, learned, and embodied by those who are in him.
22: The theme of dying with Christ applied to the old self: "put off your old self" uses the clothing/unclothing metaphor that appears in baptismal contexts (Colossians 2:11-12; Ephesians 3:9-10; Galatians 3:27). The "old self" (palaios anthrōpos — old humanity) is the pre-Christ identity shaped by the fall. Baptism into Christ's death is the moment of putting off; the daily ethical exhortation continues what the initiatory rite began. Christ's death is the death of the old humanity in those who are in him.
23: The theme of the Spirit's renewing work fulfilling the new-covenant promise: "be made new in the attitude of your minds" names the ongoing process of transformation. Jeremiah's new covenant promised a law written on hearts rather than stone (Jeremiah 31:33); Paul's renewal of the mind (also Romans 12:2) is the experiential form of that new-covenant work. The Spirit of Christ continuously conforms the believer's mind to the mind of Christ (1 Corinthians 2:16).
24: A direct fulfillment of Genesis 1:26: "put on the new self, created to be like God in true righteousness and holiness." The image of God (tselem elohim) in which Adam was made — the original righteousness and holiness — is precisely what the new self is "created" to be. The new creation in Christ (2 Corinthians 5:17) is not a novel category but the restoration of humanity's original design. To put on the new self is to put on Christ (Romans 13:14; Galatians 3:27), who is "the image of the invisible God" (Colossians 1:15).
25: The theme of covenant community ethics fulfilled in Christ: "put off falsehood and speak truthfully to your neighbor" echoes Zechariah 8:16 — the ethics of the renewed post-exilic community. Paul applies these norms to the new community of Christ because believers are "all members of one body" — the body of Christ (Ephesians 4:12), whose head is "the truth" (John 14:6). Truthful speech in the community is a participation in the character of Christ who himself is the truth.
26: The theme of redeemed human emotion: Psalms 4:4 — "in your anger do not sin" — is quoted to show that anger is not eliminated in Christ but disciplined. The emotions are not abolished by the gospel but redirected. Anger that does not find resolution before nightfall becomes a foothold for the one whom Christ defeated; anger properly bounded remains within the domain of Christ's lordship over the whole person.
27: The theme of Christ's victory over the devil requiring active vigilance from believers: Christ has disarmed the rulers and authorities (Colossians 2:15) and judged "the prince of this world" (John 12:31). But his victory does not make temptation impossible — it makes resistance possible. "Give no foothold to the devil" implies that the devil still seeks footholds precisely because he has lost the decisive battle; the defeated enemy still maneuvers in the space human sin provides.
28: The theme of Christ's generosity as the model for new-self behavior: the thief must not only stop stealing but must work "to have something to share with those in need." The movement from taking to giving mirrors Christ himself, who "became poor, so that you through his poverty might become rich" (2 Corinthians 8:9) and who "gave himself up for us" (Ephesians 5:2). The new self does not merely subtract vices; it is positively shaped by the self-giving character of Christ.
29: A revelation of God: speech "helpful for building others up according to their needs" reflects the work of the Holy Spirit, who is the Paraklētos — the one called alongside to help, to counsel, to build up (John 14:26; Ephesians 16:7-11). Believers' speech participates in the Spirit's ministry when it builds up the body. Corrupt speech (sapros — rotten, decayed) is antithetical to the Spirit's building work; it tears down what Christ is constructing.
30: A direct application of Isaiah 63:10: "do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God, with whom you were sealed for the day of redemption." Isaiah describes Israel's wilderness rebellion as grieving the Spirit of God's holiness (Isaiah 63:10) — the very Spirit who had led and sustained them. Paul applies the same warning to believers: the Spirit who seals them for the day of Christ's return (Ephesians 1:13-14) can be grieved by the very behaviors Paul is listing. The sealing is secure; the relationship within it is not beyond harm.
31: The theme of Christ's reconciling work sustaining community ethics: bitterness, rage, anger, brawling, and slander describe the hostility that Christ abolished between Jew and Gentile (Ephesians 2:14-16) and between humanity and God (Ephesians 2:16). The community gathered by Christ's peace-making cross cannot reintroduce the enmity he put to death. Putting away these vices is not mere moral improvement; it is the preservation of what Christ accomplished at infinite cost.
32: A direct Christological claim: "forgiving each other, just as in Christ God forgave you" (kathōs kai ho theos en Christō echarisato hymin). The forgiveness in the community is calibrated to the forgiveness that God enacted in Christ — not a general divine graciousness but the specific act of the cross. "In Christ" locates the forgiveness in the event of his death and resurrection. Communal forgiveness is not an ethical achievement; it is participation in and extension of the divine forgiveness that came through Christ.
